The Tonglushan ore field in the Edong district of the western Middle–Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt hosts a major Cu–Fe–Au skarn system, yet the emplacement ages, petrogenesis, and metallogenic significance of its spatially distributed intrusions remain poorly constrained. We present integrated whole-rock geochemistry, zircon UPb geochronology, zircon trace-element and Hf isotopic data, and apatite SrNd isotopic compositions for quartz monzodiorite porphyry, quartz monzodiorite, quartz diorite, and associated mafic microgranular enclaves (MMEs) from the Tonglushan ore field, together with supplementary data for the Dajipu quartz diorite from the Yangxin pluton. Zircon UPb ages indicate that the intrusive phases and their MMEs were emplaced synchronously at ca. 142–140 Ma. These rocks display high-K calc-alkaline affinities and weakly adakitic signatures, with elevated Sr/Y and (La/Yb)N ratios, and have isotopic compositions resembling those of coeval K-rich mafic rocks in the MLYRB, suggesting derivation from an enriched lithospheric mantle source followed by extensive fractional crystallization. Petrographic and geochemical evidence indicates that the MMEs formed through injection and incomplete mixing of mafic and felsic magmas, with mafic recharge supplying heat, metals, and volatiles to the evolving system. Zircon oxybarometry and zircon-based water-content estimates further show that the more strongly mineralized intrusions were generated from relatively hydrous, oxidized, and more evolved magmas, whereas the relatively barren Zhengjiawan quartz diorite records lower H2O contents and lower to more variable oxidation states. These results indicate that magma fertility in the Tonglushan ore field was jointly controlled by enriched mantle source characteristics, fractional crystallization, mafic recharge, and the development of hydrous and oxidized magmatic systems. More broadly, our results highlight whole-rock and zircon geochemical parameters as useful indicators of intrusion fertility and magmatic controls on Cu–Fe–Au mineralization in arc-related systems.

item.title: Evolution of Sulfur Concentration in the Deep Earth During the Proto‐Earth Growth: First‐Principles Study on the Sulfur Partitioning Between Metal and Silicate at High Pressure and Temperature
item.dc:creator: K. Itoh
T. Tsuchiya
item.description: Abstract Sulfur (S) is one of the promising light elements that can explain the density deficit of the core. The degree of siderophile nature of S under high pressure and temperature ( P , T ) is the key to estimating the S content in the core. However, a significant discrepancy between the metal‐silicate partition coefficient of S extrapolated from relatively low‐ P and that measured at deep mantle P , T is identified. In this study, we calculate the partition behavior of S between liquid iron and molten silicate in a wide range of P , T conditions using the ab initio thermodynamic integration molecular dynamics approach. The results indicate that S is strongly siderophile at 0–135 GPa and 3,000–5,000K, but the partition coefficient of S () markedly decreases with increasing the oxygen content in liquid iron. The pressure dependence of is also found to change around 20 GPa. These properties might reconcile the experimental conflicts and can be interpreted based on the atomic structures of the liquids. Using the derived , we model the evolution of the S concentrations in the core and mantle during the growth of the proto‐Earth. According to this model, the S concentration retained in the mantle increases as the size of the accreting planetesimals decreases and as the core becomes more oxidized. Our results further indicate that S could be a major light element in the core when the S concentration in the accreting planetesimals is larger than ∼3 mol%. Published: 2026-07-08T10:57:17+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.631 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: K. Itoh; T. Tsuchiya

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2026gc013030

Relevance: 0.631 (must_read)

Abstract Sulfur (S) is one of the promising light elements that can explain the density deficit of the core. The degree of siderophile nature of S under high pressure and temperature ( P , T ) is the key to estimating the S content in the core. However, a significant discrepancy between the metal‐silicate partition coefficient of S extrapolated from relatively low‐ P and that measured at deep mantle P , T is identified. In this study, we calculate the partition behavior of S between liquid iron and molten silicate in a wide range of P , T conditions using the ab initio thermodynamic integration molecular dynamics approach. The results indicate that S is strongly siderophile at 0–135 GPa and 3,000–5,000K, but the partition coefficient of S () markedly decreases with increasing the oxygen content in liquid iron. The pressure dependence of is also found to change around 20 GPa. These properties might reconcile the experimental conflicts and can be interpreted based on the atomic structures of the liquids. Using the derived , we model the evolution of the S concentrations in the core and mantle during the growth of the proto‐Earth. According to this model, the S concentration retained in the mantle increases as the size of the accreting planetesimals decreases and as the core becomes more oxidized. Our results further indicate that S could be a major light element in the core when the S concentration in the accreting planetesimals is larger than ∼3 mol%.

item.title: Multiple mineralization events in the eastern Central Asian orogenic belt: Insights from geology, geochronology, and geochemistry of the giant Tongshan porphyry Cu deposit, NE China
item.dc:creator: Chenglin Bai
Guiqing Xie
Junkang Zhao
Tianqi Wang
Jie Chen
item.description: The Tongshan deposit is a giant porphyry Cu system in the eastern Central Asian orogenic belt where complex alteration and mineralization processes have remained poorly constrained. This study elucidated its evolution through integrated field geology, Re-Os geochronology, and sulfur isotope analysis. Mineralization in the western segment of Tongshan is hosted in granodiorite porphyry and tonalite and is associated with phyllic and localized potassic alteration. In the eastern segment, mineralization occurs within Ordovician Duobaoshan Formation volcanic rocks and the roof pendants of granodiorite and dacite porphyry, where propylitic alteration overprints earlier alteration assemblages. Two distinct mineralization episodes were identified: an initial porphyry-style mineralization (episode A) and a later overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization (episode B). Episode A comprises three stages (A-I, A-II, and A-III), with the main Cu mineralization in stage A-II forming quartz-chalcopyrite ± molybdenite ± pyrite veinlets with K-feldspar halos. Episode B veins overprinted earlier veins and contributed significant additional Cu through four stages (B-I, B-II, B-III, and B-IV) of wider quartz-sulfide veining. Systematic logging showed a positive correlation between the density of overprinting veins and Cu(-Mo-Au) grades. Molybdenite Re-Os dating suggests that episode A and B veins likely occurred at 475.6 ± 2.3 Ma and 228.1 ± 2.9 Ma, respectively. These ages suggest that the porphyry-style mineralization corresponds to the Ordovician granodiorite porphyry intrusions, whereas the overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization was associated with Triassic granodiorite and dacite porphyry. A significant characteristic at Tongshan is the prolonged interval of more than 200 m.y. between the two mineralization episodes. The δ34S values of the episode A sulfides range from −2.1‰ to −0.5‰, which is consistent with a magmatic source. The episode B sulfide δ34S signatures of −5.6‰ to +0.9‰ are analogous to those of a mantle-crust mixed source, indicating the Duobaoshan Formation may have been a potential sulfur reservoir. These results provide a refined genetic model for Tongshan and valuable insights for exploring other Paleozoic porphyry Cu deposits in the Central Asian orogenic belt. Published: 2026-07-09T21:55:56+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.617 (must_read)

Two distinct mineralization episodes were identified: an initial porphyry-style mineralization (episode A) and a later overprinting porphyry(-epithermal) mineralization (episode B). Episode A comprises three stages (A-I, A-II, and A-III), with the main Cu mineralization in stage A-II forming quartz-chalcopyrite ± molybdenite ± pyrite veinlets with K-feldspar halos. Episode B veins overprinted earlier veins and contributed significant additional Cu through four stages (B-I, B-II, B-III, and B-IV) of wider quartz-sulfide veining. Systematic logging showed a positive correlation between the density of overprinting veins and Cu(-Mo-Au) grades. A significant characteristic at Tongshan is the prolonged interval of more than 200 m.y. between the two mineralization episodes. item.title: Paleoproterozoic Dongyaozhuang orogenic gold deposit in the Trans-North China Orogen: Constraints from in-situ U-Th-Pb dating of hydrothermal monazite and trace elements and sulfur isotopes of sulfides
item.dc:creator: Ying Ma
Shao-Yong Jiang
Ning Jin
Dao-Hui Pi
Yu Jiang
Guo-Hai Guo
item.description: The Wutai Complex in the Trans-North China Orogen hosts several significant Precambrian mesothermal, greenstone-hosted gold deposits. However, as in many Precambrian orogenic belts worldwide, these deposits are associated with complex hydrothermal systems involving multistage fluid activity during successive mineralization stages. As a result, their age, origin, evolutionary history, and genetic mechanisms remain poorly constrained. Here, we present in-situ hydrothermal monazite U-Th-Pb geochronological data, together with trace-element and S isotope analyses of multiple generations of pyrite and arsenopyrite, from the representative greenstone-hosted Dongyaozhuang gold deposit in the Wutai Complex. These integrated datasets provide new constraints on the hydrothermal evolution and genesis of the deposit. Gold mineralization occurred in three distinct stages and is represented by veinlet-hosted and disseminated ores: Stage I disseminated pyrite-arsenopyrite assemblages, Stage II quartz-carbonate-pyrite veinlets, and Stage III quartz-chalcopyrite-pyrite veinlets. Six generations of pyrite were identified, including Py1 from Stage I, Py2a, Py2b, and Py2c from Stage II, and Py3a and Py3b from Stage III. Hydrothermal monazite from Stage II gold-mineralized veinlets yielded a mean 232Th/208Pb age of 1846.7 ± 4.7 Ma, which is interpreted as the timing of gold mineralization. This age is inconsistent with regional granitic magmatism at 2560–2515 Ma, 2180–2120 Ma, and ∼1800 Ma, but overlaps with metamorphic and deformational processes related to the Paleoproterozoic orogeny. Most of the gold was precipitated during Stage II, when pyrite commonly developed a complex core-mantle-rim texture. Most sulfide generations show a narrow range of δ34SV-CDT values from −3.7 ‰ to 2.6 ‰, consistent with sulfur mobilization from greenstone wall rocks and its subsequent reprecipitation at the site of ore formation. In contrast, the negative δ34SV-CDT values of Py2a (−6.7 to −2.3 ‰) suggest isotopic fractionation induced by an increase in the pH of the metamorphic ore-forming fluid. These results, together with the geological evidence, indicate that the Dongyaozhuang deposit is an orogenic gold deposit genetically related to regional metamorphism and orogenic deformation associated with the Paleoproterozoic continent-arc-continent collision between the Western and Eastern Blocks. Published: 2026-07-04T01:02:54+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.606 (must_read)

These integrated datasets provide new constraints on the hydrothermal evolution and genesis of the deposit. Gold mineralization occurred in three distinct stages and is represented by veinlet-hosted and disseminated ores: Stage I disseminated pyrite-arsenopyrite assemblages, Stage II quartz-carbonate-pyrite veinlets, and Stage III quartz-chalcopyrite-pyrite veinlets. Six generations of pyrite were identified, including Py1 from Stage I, Py2a, Py2b, and Py2c from Stage II, and Py3a and Py3b from Stage III. Hydrothermal monazite from Stage II gold-mineralized veinlets yielded a mean 232Th/208Pb age of 1846.7 ± 4.7 Ma, which is interpreted as the timing of gold mineralization. This age is inconsistent with regional granitic magmatism at 2560–2515 Ma, 2180–2120 Ma, and ∼1800 Ma, but overlaps with metamorphic and deformational processes related to the Paleoproterozoic orogeny. Most of the gold was precipitated during Stage II, when pyrite commonly developed a complex core-mantle-rim texture. Most sulfide generations show a narrow range of δ34SV-CDT values from −3.7 ‰ to 2.6 ‰, consistent with sulfur mobilization from greenstone wall rocks and its subsequent reprecipitation at the site of ore formation. In contrast, the negative δ34SV-CDT values of Py2a (−6.7 to −2.3 ‰) suggest isotopic fractionation induced by an increase in the pH of the metamorphic ore-forming fluid. These results, together with the geological evidence, indicate that the Dongyaozhuang deposit is an orogenic gold deposit genetically related to regional metamorphism and orogenic deformation associated with the Paleoproterozoic continent-arc-continent collision between the Western and Eastern Blocks.

item.title: An early jurassic gold mineralization event in the Liaodong Peninsula, northeastern margin of the North China Craton: evidence from two types of magmatic and hydrothermal apatite in the Linjia deposit
item.dc:creator: Wanyi Feng
Jiahao Zheng
Bin Chen
Shuaijie Liu
item.description: Apatite is a widespread accessory mineral that occurs frequently in magmatic rocks and diverse hydrothermal ore deposits, and its textural features, geochronology, and geochemical signatures offer critical constraints on magmatic activity and metasomatic events. Here, two types of magmatic and hydrothermal apatite were identified by CL imaging in the hydrothermal altered auriferous granite in the Linjia gold deposit, Liaodong Peninsula, northeastern part of the North China Craton (NCC). The magmatic apatite (Ap1) grains with bright CL imaging formed earlier than the hydrothermal apatite (Ap2) crystals with dark CL imaging. The U-Pb dating results suggest the Ap1 formed contemporaneously with magmatic zircon in the granite at ca. 231 Ma, and these Triassic dates define the emplacement age of the ore-hosting granite. By contrast, the Ap2 and hydrothermal rutiles in the mineralized granite and schist both formed at ca. 186 Ma. The hydrothermal Ap2 grains show much higher S contents than the Ap1, suggesting they may be precipitated contemporaneously with sulfides from the S-bearing hydrothermal fluids. In addition, the calculated δ18Ofluid values, relatively high U as well as Th contents, and REE pattern of Ap2 are clearly distinct from metamorphic fluids, but comparable to magmatic‑hydrothermal fluids derived from granitic magmas. The textural, geochemical, and isotopic evidence indicates that Ap2 and rutiles formed from hydrothermal fluids during gold precipitation, and their Early Jurassic U-Pb ages represent the timing of gold mineralization. Based on the above evidence, we infer that Linjia represents an intrusion-related gold deposit related to a buried Early Jurassic felsic intrusion at depth. Importantly, the newly identified gold mineralization in the Linjia deposit coincides with the onset of paleo-Pacific plate subduction beneath the NCC, suggesting that there was a relatively small-scale Early Jurassic gold mineralization related to subduction prior to the ca. 120 Ma large-scale gold accumulation event in the NCC. This study highlights the importance of combining the microtextures, geochronological data, trace elements, and isotopic compositions of individual apatite grains to unravel the genesis of cryptic magmatic and hydrothermal apatite and reconstruct obscure geological events within magmatic-hydrothermal systems. Published: 2026-07-08T07:06:01+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.606 (must_read)

Apatite is a widespread accessory mineral that occurs frequently in magmatic rocks and diverse hydrothermal ore deposits, and its textural features, geochronology, and geochemical signatures offer critical constraints on magmatic activity and metasomatic events. Here, two types of magmatic and hydrothermal apatite were identified by CL imaging in the hydrothermal altered auriferous granite in the Linjia gold deposit, Liaodong Peninsula, northeastern part of the North China Craton (NCC). The U-Pb dating results suggest the Ap1 formed contemporaneously with magmatic zircon in the granite at ca. 231 Ma, and these Triassic dates define the emplacement age of the ore-hosting granite. By contrast, the Ap2 and hydrothermal rutiles in the mineralized granite and schist both formed at ca. 186 Ma. The hydrothermal Ap2 grains show much higher S contents than the Ap1, suggesting they may be precipitated contemporaneously with sulfides from the S-bearing hydrothermal fluids. In addition, the calculated δ18Ofluid values, relatively high U as well as Th contents, and REE pattern of Ap2 are clearly distinct from metamorphic fluids, but comparable to magmatic‑hydrothermal fluids derived from granitic magmas. The textural, geochemical, and isotopic evidence indicates that Ap2 and rutiles formed from hydrothermal fluids during gold precipitation, and their Early Jurassic U-Pb ages represent the timing of gold mineralization. Based on the above evidence, we infer that Linjia represents an intrusion-related gold deposit related to a buried Early Jurassic felsic intrusion at depth. Importantly, the newly identified gold mineralization in the Linjia deposit coincides with the onset of paleo-Pacific plate subduction beneath the NCC, suggesting that there was a relatively small-scale Early Jurassic gold mineralization related to subduction prior to the ca. 120 Ma large-scale gold accumulation event in the NCC. This study highlights the importance of combining the microtextures, geochronological data, trace elements, and isotopic compositions of individual apatite grains to unravel the genesis of cryptic magmatic and hydrothermal apatite and reconstruct obscure geological events within magmatic-hydrothermal systems.

item.title: Sulfur and lead isotope geochemistry of sulfide minerals from the Yushui copper deposit, eastern Guangdong, South China
item.dc:creator: Gang Chen
Changhui Ke
Junzeng Zuo
Zhihui Dai
Maohong Chen
item.description: The Yushui volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS) copper deposit in eastern Guangdong, South China, is distinguished by two discrete mineralization events: a Carboniferous stratiform ore system (ca. 308 Ma) and a Jurassic vein-type overprint (ca. 163 Ma). In situ sulfur and lead isotope analyses (LA‑MC‑ICP‑MS) were conducted on sulfides from both ore types to constrain sulfur and metal sources and hydrothermal system evolution. Sulfur isotopes (δ34S = − 23.88‰ to + 4.01‰) show systematic variations: early exhalative and stockwork ores record significant bacterial and thermochemical reduction of seawater sulfate, whereas main‑stage massive and banded ores approach magmatic sulfur values (0‰ to + 3‰). Vein‑type ores yield δ34S indicative of a dominant magmatic source linked to Jurassic igneous activity. Lead isotopes form three linear groups corresponding to Carboniferous VMS mineralization, Jurassic vein‑type overprint, and host volcanic rocks. Lead in stratiform ores was largely leached from basement crust with minor magmatic input, while vein‑type ores predominantly inherited lead from pre‑existing stratiform ores in a closed system. These results confirm a two‑stage genesis: (1) shallow‑marine seafloor hydrothermal sedimentation forming primary VMS ore bodies at ∼ 308 Ma; (2) Jurassic magmatic‑hydrothermal reworking and superimposition generating vein‑type ore bodies at ∼ 163 Ma. This study highlights the utility of in situ S–Pb isotopes in unravelling polyphase ore‑forming processes in VMS deposits modified by later tectono‑magmatic events. Published: 2026-07-08T07:06:10+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.604 (consider)

The Yushui volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS) copper deposit in eastern Guangdong, South China, is distinguished by two discrete mineralization events: a Carboniferous stratiform ore system (ca. 308 Ma) and a Jurassic vein-type overprint (ca. 163 Ma). In situ sulfur and lead isotope analyses (LA‑MC‑ICP‑MS) were conducted on sulfides from both ore types to constrain sulfur and metal sources and hydrothermal system evolution. Sulfur isotopes (δ34S = − 23.88‰ to + 4.01‰) show systematic variations: early exhalative and stockwork ores record significant bacterial and thermochemical reduction of seawater sulfate, whereas main‑stage massive and banded ores approach magmatic sulfur values (0‰ to + 3‰). Vein‑type ores yield δ34S indicative of a dominant magmatic source linked to Jurassic igneous activity. Lead isotopes form three linear groups corresponding to Carboniferous VMS mineralization, Jurassic vein‑type overprint, and host volcanic rocks. Lead in stratiform ores was largely leached from basement crust with minor magmatic input, while vein‑type ores predominantly inherited lead from pre‑existing stratiform ores in a closed system. These results confirm a two‑stage genesis: (1) shallow‑marine seafloor hydrothermal sedimentation forming primary VMS ore bodies at ∼ 308 Ma; (2) Jurassic magmatic‑hydrothermal reworking and superimposition generating vein‑type ore bodies at ∼ 163 Ma. This study highlights the utility of in situ S–Pb isotopes in unravelling polyphase ore‑forming processes in VMS deposits modified by later tectono‑magmatic events.

item.title: Gold mineralisation characteristics of the typical ore body and the potential for exploration in the Qiucun Au deposit, Dehua county, Fujian province, China
item.dc:creator: Fei-Peng Fan
Pei Ni
Fan Xiao
Yan Zhou
Guang-Fu Xing
Zi-Hao Zhao
Zhi-Qiang Yu
Ming-Seng Fan
Nan Wang
item.description: The Qiucun Au deposit is located in Dehua County, Fujian Province, within the Wuyi Mountain metallogenic belt. Gold mineralisation of the typical ore body exhibits distinct zoning characteristics between 940 m and 647 m, is structurally controlled by a volcanic conduit and its associated fracture zones, and is closely associated with quartz (chalcedony)-sericite-pyrite-calcite assemblages. To decipher the ore-forming processes of the Qiucun deposit, detailed geological investigations were carried out, along with sulfur isotope analysis of syn-ore pyrite, microthermometric analysis (homogenization temperature and salinity) of fluid inclusions in syn-ore quartz, and U-Pb dating of syn-ore calcite. The results show that in typical orebodies, gold grade increases and then decreases from shallow to deeper sections. Gold occurs mainly as fracture-fillings within pyrite, displaying diverse morphologies and colours that correlate with grade. Significant gold mineralisation is hosted within sericite alteration zones, whereas the presence of calcite is generally associated with lower gold grades. The δ 34 S values of gold-related pyrite range from –6.8‰ to + 1.6‰, suggesting a deep magmatic source for the ore-forming materials. Fluid inclusions in quartz are dominated by two-phase (liquid-rich) types with minor evidence of boiling assemblages, obtaining low-temperature (180–260 °C) and low-salinity (0.35–5.56 wt% NaCl equiv.). U-Pb dating of calcite yields an age of 152.0 ± 17 Ma. Integrated evidence indicates that the Qiucun low-sulfidation epithermal Au deposit is related to the subduction of the paleo-Pacific Plate. Fractured volcanic breccia zones served as the main pathways for hydrothermal fluid migration and ore precipitation. Pyrite, sericite, calcite, and other minerals at different elevations are the main indicators for further gold exploration in the Qiucun gold deposit and its adjacent areas. Published: 2026-07-06T23:29:31+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.600 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107420

Relevance: 0.600 (consider)

The Qiucun Au deposit is located in Dehua County, Fujian Province, within the Wuyi Mountain metallogenic belt. Gold mineralisation of the typical ore body exhibits distinct zoning characteristics between 940 m and 647 m, is structurally controlled by a volcanic conduit and its associated fracture zones, and is closely associated with quartz (chalcedony)-sericite-pyrite-calcite assemblages. To decipher the ore-forming processes of the Qiucun deposit, detailed geological investigations were carried out, along with sulfur isotope analysis of syn-ore pyrite, microthermometric analysis (homogenization temperature and salinity) of fluid inclusions in syn-ore quartz, and U-Pb dating of syn-ore calcite. The results show that in typical orebodies, gold grade increases and then decreases from shallow to deeper sections. Gold occurs mainly as fracture-fillings within pyrite, displaying diverse morphologies and colours that correlate with grade. Significant gold mineralisation is hosted within sericite alteration zones, whereas the presence of calcite is generally associated with lower gold grades. The δ 34 S values of gold-related pyrite range from –6.8‰ to + 1.6‰, suggesting a deep magmatic source for the ore-forming materials. Fluid inclusions in quartz are dominated by two-phase (liquid-rich) types with minor evidence of boiling assemblages, obtaining low-temperature (180–260 °C) and low-salinity (0.35–5.56 wt% NaCl equiv.). U-Pb dating of calcite yields an age of 152.0 ± 17 Ma. Integrated evidence indicates that the Qiucun low-sulfidation epithermal Au deposit is related to the subduction of the paleo-Pacific Plate. Fractured volcanic breccia zones served as the main pathways for hydrothermal fluid migration and ore precipitation. Pyrite, sericite, calcite, and other minerals at different elevations are the main indicators for further gold exploration in the Qiucun gold deposit and its adjacent areas.

item.title: Niobium and heavy rare earth element mineralization in the Bayan Obo deposit, China: insights from aeschynite mineralogy, geochemistry, and geochronology
item.dc:creator: Qing Sun
Xian-Hua Li
item.description: The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content. Published: 2026-07-04T22:23:19+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.598 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qing Sun; Xian-Hua Li

Journal: Geoscience Frontiers

DOI: 10.1016/j.gsf.2026.102405

Relevance: 0.598 (consider)

The Bayan Obo deposit in China is the largest light rare earth element (LREE) deposit worldwide, and also hosts substantial Nb and heavy REE (HREE) resources. However, the timing and enrichment mechanisms of the Nb and HREE mineralization are poorly constrained. Aeschynite is the most important Nb- and HREE-bearing mineral at Bayan Obo and was investigated in this study to reconstruct the history of Nb and HREE mineralization. In situ Lu–Hf isochron dating of two types of aeschynite yielded consistent ages of 435–420 Ma, which overlap with the Th–Pb isotopic ages of intergrown monazite (438–392 Ma). These results demonstrate that early Paleozoic is a significant episode for Nb and HREE mineralization. The aeschynite has a close spatiotemporal relationship with alkali silicates, suggesting that hydrothermal fluids were naturally rich in alkalis and dominantly exsolved from coeval carbonatitic magmas. Strontium isotopic compositions of aeschynite are relatively enriched and exhibit an increased trend from the type-1 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7037–0.7064) to type-2 (87Sr/86Sr = 0.7072–0.7128), which was likely caused by heterogeneous crustal contamination during the evolution of hydrothermal fluids. Paragenetic aeschynite, calcite, fluorite and bastnäsite reflect that carbonate (CO3 2–) and fluoride (F-) anions played a key role for Nb mobilization. The unloading of Nb in aeschynite was usually related to the variation of Ti content in hydrothermal fluids. According to apparent correlation of REE, Th and Ca in aeschynite and developed ilmenite, Nb unloading was influenced by the processes of coupled substitution or ilmenite crystallization. The enrichment of HREE was ascribed to preferential LREE fractionation following gradual temperature decrease of CO3 2–-bearing alkaline hydrothermal fluids. Moreover, earlier crystallized LREE minerals is potential trigger for local elevation of HREE content.

item.title: Natural CO2 sequestration: mechanisms and conditions of carbonate precipitation in basalt
item.dc:creator: M.P. Rosenqvist
K.G. Dunkel
L.P. Nogueira
S. Planke
S. Polteau
M. Ardit
F. Nestola
L. Menegon
item.description: Carbon sequestration in mafic and ultramafic rocks has been demonstrated as a safe and efficient storage method; however, the specifics of the carbonation reactions remain uncertain, especially in volcanic reservoirs. In this study, we contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms and conditions of natural CO2 sequestration in basalt by analysing 43 early Eocene basalt samples from IODP boreholes U1571A and U1572A on the Skoll High on the mid-Norwegian continental margin. Using high-resolution 2D and 3D imaging and microanalytical techniques together with X-ray powder diffraction, U-Pb dating, as well as carbon and oxygen isotope analysis, we identified two main types of carbonate precipitation: 1) direct precipitation in vesicles, and 2) replacement of olivine and clinopyroxene by coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. While most vesicles are coated with smectite, this did not inhibit fluid flow and carbonate precipitation. Mineral parageneses, δ13C ratios, and δ18O ratios record carbonate precipitation in the vesicles at < 100˚C and suggest a variation of carbon sources, from early intruded meteoric water mixed with volcanic and thermogenic carbon to marine sourced carbon. Vesicle carbonate comprises up to 8.6 vol% of its host basalt (as determined by μ-CT image analysis) and replacement carbonate 0.6 – 0.94 wt% (as determined by powder diffraction). We estimate that within the upper 100 m of the subaerially emplaced basalt sequence on Skoll High, approximately 1 Mt of CO2 is stored as carbonate per km2 area, at least 0.3 Mt of which has precipitated in the microporous basalt through coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. This illustrates that the storage potential of basaltic rocks is not limited to the macro-porosity (vesicles) but may be significantly enhanced by replacement reactions. Published: 2026-07-09T12:39:23+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.597 (consider)

Authors: M.P. Rosenqvist; K.G. Dunkel; L.P. Nogueira; S. Planke; S. Polteau; M. Ardit; F. Nestola; L. Menegon

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.07.013

Relevance: 0.597 (consider)

Carbon sequestration in mafic and ultramafic rocks has been demonstrated as a safe and efficient storage method; however, the specifics of the carbonation reactions remain uncertain, especially in volcanic reservoirs. In this study, we contribute to a deeper understanding of the mechanisms and conditions of natural CO2 sequestration in basalt by analysing 43 early Eocene basalt samples from IODP boreholes U1571A and U1572A on the Skoll High on the mid-Norwegian continental margin. Using high-resolution 2D and 3D imaging and microanalytical techniques together with X-ray powder diffraction, U-Pb dating, as well as carbon and oxygen isotope analysis, we identified two main types of carbonate precipitation: 1) direct precipitation in vesicles, and 2) replacement of olivine and clinopyroxene by coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. While most vesicles are coated with smectite, this did not inhibit fluid flow and carbonate precipitation. Mineral parageneses, δ13C ratios, and δ18O ratios record carbonate precipitation in the vesicles at < 100˚C and suggest a variation of carbon sources, from early intruded meteoric water mixed with volcanic and thermogenic carbon to marine sourced carbon. Vesicle carbonate comprises up to 8.6 vol% of its host basalt (as determined by μ-CT image analysis) and replacement carbonate 0.6 – 0.94 wt% (as determined by powder diffraction). We estimate that within the upper 100 m of the subaerially emplaced basalt sequence on Skoll High, approximately 1 Mt of CO2 is stored as carbonate per km2 area, at least 0.3 Mt of which has precipitated in the microporous basalt through coupled dissolution-precipitation reactions. This illustrates that the storage potential of basaltic rocks is not limited to the macro-porosity (vesicles) but may be significantly enhanced by replacement reactions.

item.title: Paleoproterozoic orogenic gold system in the Contendas-Mirante belt, São Francisco Craton, Brazil: Insights from lithogeochemistry, U–Pb detrital zircon geochronology, and geodynamic analysis
item.dc:creator: Ricardo Ramos Spreafico
Astrid Holzheid
Elson Paiva Oliveira
Carlos Marcello Dias Fernandes
Carlos Eduardo Ganade de Araujo
Fabiano Cruz do Livramento
Antônio Marcos Vitória de Moraes
item.description: Orogenic gold systems are important sources of Paleoproterozoic gold mineralization in cratonic settings worldwide and are commonly associated with convergent-margin tectonics and crustal-scale fluid flow. In the northern São Francisco Craton, eastern Brazil, several Rhyacian-Orosirian gold occurrences are known; however, the geodynamic context and structural framework controlling mineralization in the Contendas-Mirante volcanosedimentary belt remain poorly constrained. This study integrates geological and structural mapping, core sample analysis, whole-rock geochemistry, U–Pb detrital zircon geochronology, and aerogeophysical data to constrain the tectonic setting and mineralization processes of the Contendas-Mirante belt. Metabasalts of the Neoarchean lower unit display light rare earth element-enriched patterns, elevated Th and U contents, and pronounced negative Nb–Ta–Ti anomalies, consistent with a continental geochemical signature. Elevated Th/Yb, La/Yb, and Th/Nb ratios may have been partly modified by secondary processes and are therefore considered supportive, but not restricted to an arc-like continental geochemical signature, as they may also reflect crustal contamination during the evolution of basaltic magma. U–Pb detrital zircon ages from associated phyllites yield a maximum depositional age of 2626 ± 31 Ma, indicating provenance from proximal Neoarchean volcanic sources along a continental margin. Structural analyses reveal a dominant north–south-trending crustal-scale lineament formed during the Rhyacian-Orosirian tectonothermal event, itself associated with the accretion of oceanic and continental arcs and older continental blocks in the northern São Francisco Craton. Subordinate northwest-southeast and northeast-southwest structures controlled the distribution of lithologies and fluid migration. Gold mineralization in the Contendas-Mirante belt is hosted by brecciated ferruginous metacherts of the lower unit and is spatially associated with quartz-sericite schists, reflecting fluid focusing within brittle-ductile deformation zones where iron-rich sedimentary rocks acted as effective chemical and rheological traps. The results support a tectonic evolution involving Neoarchean continental-margin volcanism, followed by the Rhyacian-Orosirian tectonothermal event and orogenic gold mineralization, most likely within a continental arc system. The Contendas-Mirante belt mineralization exhibits key temporal, structural, and geodynamic characteristics of Paleoproterozoic orogenic gold systems in the Congo, Amazonian, and West African cratons, contributing to broader reconstructions of crustal growth, metallogenesis, and gold exploration models in the São Francisco Craton and analogous cratonic provinces worldwide. Published: 2026-07-10T19:28:39+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.595 (consider)

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108210

item.title: Differential exhumation of giant lode gold deposits in the Jiaodong Peninsula, eastern China
item.dc:creator: Hongtao Zhao
Yu Zhang
Lin Gong
Martin Danišík
Yongjun Shao
Yuanming Pan
Yanbo Xu
Teimoor Nazari-Dehkordi
Yuzhou Feng
item.description: The Jiaodong Peninsula is China’s premier gold province, containing >5500 t of gold reserves, and exhibits pronounced spatial variation in lode gold mineralization. This variation is especially evident along the Jiaojia fault zone, where the northern segment hosts >90% of the gold endowment, while the southern segment is only sparsely mineralized. To understand this pattern, we integrate new apatite (U-Th)/He (AHe; n = 48 single-grain ages from eight samples, 50.1 ± 9.6 Ma to 2.8 ± 0.7 Ma) and zircon (U-Th)/He (ZHe; n = 40 single-grain ages from eight samples, 133.8 ± 6.1 Ma to 106.8 ± 5.0 Ma) ages obtained from a vertical drill-core profile (Dayinjia ZK32-1; surface to −1492 m depth) in the southern Jiaojia fault zone with a comprehensive low-temperature thermochronologic database (70 zircon fission-track [ZFT], 164 apatite fission-track [AFT], 84 ZHe, and 52 AHe). Thermal modeling reveals five distinct exhumation phases (120−95 Ma, 95−60 Ma, 60−45 Ma, 45−15 Ma, and 15 Ma to present) occurring in the southern segment. Regional analysis reveals substantially less post-mineralization exhumation along the southern Jiaojia fault zone relative to the northern segment. This disparity is likely responsible for the lower gold endowment found in the south. Regionally, two major post-mineralization exhumation episodes (110−70 Ma and 50−30 Ma) in the Jiaodong Peninsula reflect tectonic transitions driven by evolving subduction dynamics. These episodes indicate elevated exhumation rates and greater cumulative denudation in the eastern peninsula. This likely limited ore preservation, contributing to the heterogeneous distribution of gold resources across the peninsula. Notably, the older thermochronologic ages at the low elevations of key gold districts, such as Sanshandao, Jiaojia, and Linglong, suggest limited post-mineralization exhumation, which has contributed to the preservation of these ore deposits. These findings underscore the critical relationship between exhumation processes and the spatial variation in gold mineralization and demonstrate that low-temperature thermochronology is an effective tool for guiding the exploration of deep-lying lode gold deposits. Published: 2026-07-10T20:14:07+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.593 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hongtao Zhao; Yu Zhang; Lin Gong; Martin Danišík; Yongjun Shao; Yuanming Pan; Yanbo Xu; Teimoor Nazari-Dehkordi; Yuzhou Feng

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38806.1

Relevance: 0.593 (consider)

The Jiaodong Peninsula is China’s premier gold province, containing >5500 t of gold reserves, and exhibits pronounced spatial variation in lode gold mineralization. This variation is especially evident along the Jiaojia fault zone, where the northern segment hosts >90% of the gold endowment, while the southern segment is only sparsely mineralized. To understand this pattern, we integrate new apatite (U-Th)/He (AHe; n = 48 single-grain ages from eight samples, 50.1 ± 9.6 Ma to 2.8 ± 0.7 Ma) and zircon (U-Th)/He (ZHe; n = 40 single-grain ages from eight samples, 133.8 ± 6.1 Ma to 106.8 ± 5.0 Ma) ages obtained from a vertical drill-core profile (Dayinjia ZK32-1; surface to −1492 m depth) in the southern Jiaojia fault zone with a comprehensive low-temperature thermochronologic database (70 zircon fission-track [ZFT], 164 apatite fission-track [AFT], 84 ZHe, and 52 AHe). Thermal modeling reveals five distinct exhumation phases (120−95 Ma, 95−60 Ma, 60−45 Ma, 45−15 Ma, and 15 Ma to present) occurring in the southern segment. Regional analysis reveals substantially less post-mineralization exhumation along the southern Jiaojia fault zone relative to the northern segment. This disparity is likely responsible for the lower gold endowment found in the south. Regionally, two major post-mineralization exhumation episodes (110−70 Ma and 50−30 Ma) in the Jiaodong Peninsula reflect tectonic transitions driven by evolving subduction dynamics. These episodes indicate elevated exhumation rates and greater cumulative denudation in the eastern peninsula. This likely limited ore preservation, contributing to the heterogeneous distribution of gold resources across the peninsula. Notably, the older thermochronologic ages at the low elevations of key gold districts, such as Sanshandao, Jiaojia, and Linglong, suggest limited post-mineralization exhumation, which has contributed to the preservation of these ore deposits. These findings underscore the critical relationship between exhumation processes and the spatial variation in gold mineralization and demonstrate that low-temperature thermochronology is an effective tool for guiding the exploration of deep-lying lode gold deposits.

item.title: Repeated recharge of primitive melts in the lower oceanic crust: Constraints from the Dongco ophiolite, Central Tibet
item.dc:creator: Xiao-Ni Li
Chuan-Zhou Liu
Wei-Qi Zhang
Tong Liu
Chang Zhang
Yin-Zheng Lin
Zhen Zhang
item.description: Magma recharge and associated melt–mush interaction can substantially modify the composition of the lower oceanic crust. However, the mineralogical and geochemical characteristics of such interaction remain poorly constrained. Here, we present petrological, geochronological, and geochemical data for poikilitic olivine gabbros, layered olivine gabbros and layered gabbros from the Dongco ophiolite in central Tibet. Zircon U-Pb ages and mineral Sr-Nd isotopes indicate that the poikilitic olivine gabbros and their host layered gabbros were coeval and derived from the same magmatic system. Both the poikilitic olivine gabbros and the layered olivine gabbros record evidence of melt–mush interaction. The poikilitic olivine gabbros display clear disequilibrium textures, including clinopyroxene oikocrysts enclosing plagioclase chadacrysts and vermicular orthopyroxene surrounding olivine grains. The clinopyroxene oikocrysts exhibit oscillatory and normal Cr zoning from core to rim. In contrast, the layered olivine gabbros lack conspicuous reaction textures, whereas clinopyroxene in these rocks preserves oscillatory, normal, and reverse Cr zoning, indicating cryptic melt–mush interaction. These textural and mineral-chemical features indicate that both the poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros recorded melt–mush interaction, but at different levels of textural expression. In addition, clinopyroxene grains from both poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros are characterized by low TiO2 contents and the absence of significant core-to-rim variations in incompatible trace elements, suggesting that the reacting melt was primitive rather than evolved. Assimilation-fractionation-crystallization modelling further indicates that clinopyroxene oikocrysts in the poikilitic olivine gabbros formed under melt-dominated reaction conditions, with limited assimilation of pre-existing mush components, consistent with their coarse grain sizes. In contrast, clinopyroxene in the layered olivine gabbros records a larger contribution from assimilated or reacted mush components relative to crystallization, indicating stronger mush-buffered modification. These results demonstrate that repeated recharge of primitive melts can modify mineral compositions and geochemical signatures of gabbroic cumulates, thereby contributing to the chemical evolution of the lower oceanic crust. Authors: Xiao-Ni Li; Chuan-Zhou Liu; Wei-Qi Zhang; Tong Liu; Chang Zhang; Yin-Zheng Lin; Zhen Zhang

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108709

Relevance: 0.592 (consider)

Magma recharge and associated melt–mush interaction can substantially modify the composition of the lower oceanic crust. However, the mineralogical and geochemical characteristics of such interaction remain poorly constrained. Here, we present petrological, geochronological, and geochemical data for poikilitic olivine gabbros, layered olivine gabbros and layered gabbros from the Dongco ophiolite in central Tibet. Zircon U-Pb ages and mineral Sr-Nd isotopes indicate that the poikilitic olivine gabbros and their host layered gabbros were coeval and derived from the same magmatic system. Both the poikilitic olivine gabbros and the layered olivine gabbros record evidence of melt–mush interaction. The poikilitic olivine gabbros display clear disequilibrium textures, including clinopyroxene oikocrysts enclosing plagioclase chadacrysts and vermicular orthopyroxene surrounding olivine grains. The clinopyroxene oikocrysts exhibit oscillatory and normal Cr zoning from core to rim. In contrast, the layered olivine gabbros lack conspicuous reaction textures, whereas clinopyroxene in these rocks preserves oscillatory, normal, and reverse Cr zoning, indicating cryptic melt–mush interaction. These textural and mineral-chemical features indicate that both the poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros recorded melt–mush interaction, but at different levels of textural expression. In addition, clinopyroxene grains from both poikilitic and layered olivine gabbros are characterized by low TiO2 contents and the absence of significant core-to-rim variations in incompatible trace elements, suggesting that the reacting melt was primitive rather than evolved. Assimilation-fractionation-crystallization modelling further indicates that clinopyroxene oikocrysts in the poikilitic olivine gabbros formed under melt-dominated reaction conditions, with limited assimilation of pre-existing mush components, consistent with their coarse grain sizes. In contrast, clinopyroxene in the layered olivine gabbros records a larger contribution from assimilated or reacted mush components relative to crystallization, indicating stronger mush-buffered modification. These results demonstrate that repeated recharge of primitive melts can modify mineral compositions and geochemical signatures of gabbroic cumulates, thereby contributing to the chemical evolution of the lower oceanic crust.

item.title: Tracing the Origin and Hydrothermal Overprint of Magnetite-Apatite Mineralization in the New Afton Cu-Au ± PGE Alkalic Porphyry Deposit: Insights from Magnetite Textures and Chemistry
item.dc:creator: Andres Felipe Gonzalez-Duran
Adam C. Simon
Robert Holder
John F.H. Thompson
Devin Wade
Catherine Ryan
Ryan Mathur
item.description: Abstract Alkalic porphyry deposits are significant sources of Cu-Au ± platinum group elements (PGE), with magnetite being particularly abundant in the Triassic alkalic systems of British Columbia, Canada. The New Afton area hosts both Cu-Au ± PGE mineralization and several massive magnetite lodes, which were historically mined for iron. The origin of these magnetite orebodies and their relationship to alkalic porphyry mineralization remains unclear. This study integrates field observations and magnetite chemistry and textures to elucidate their formation. We identify two stages of magnetite mineralization: an early high-temperature (>500°C) magmatic-hydrothermal stage, characterized by ilmenite exsolution lamellae, high-Ti magnetite, and symplectitic intergrowths with ilmenite, followed by a later stage of hydrothermal reequilibration that precipitated ilmenite-free Ti-depleted magnetite. Rather than preserving its original crystallization signature, magnetite records a complex history of hydrothermal activity, including Cu-Au-PGE-bearing magmatic-hydrothermal fluid circulation and repeated, superimposed coupled dissolution-reprecipitation events. These findings align with those from other magmatic-hydrothermal ore systems such as iron oxide copper-gold and Kiruna-type iron oxide-apatite deposits, emphasizing the widespread nature of magnetite reequilibration and the need for caution when using magnetite as a petrogenetic indicator. Published: 2026-07-08T19:38:19+00:00 Venue: Economic Geology Score: 0.588 (consider)

Abstract Alkalic porphyry deposits are significant sources of Cu-Au ± platinum group elements (PGE), with magnetite being particularly abundant in the Triassic alkalic systems of British Columbia, Canada. The New Afton area hosts both Cu-Au ± PGE mineralization and several massive magnetite lodes, which were historically mined for iron. The origin of these magnetite orebodies and their relationship to alkalic porphyry mineralization remains unclear. This study integrates field observations and magnetite chemistry and textures to elucidate their formation. We identify two stages of magnetite mineralization: an early high-temperature (>500°C) magmatic-hydrothermal stage, characterized by ilmenite exsolution lamellae, high-Ti magnetite, and symplectitic intergrowths with ilmenite, followed by a later stage of hydrothermal reequilibration that precipitated ilmenite-free Ti-depleted magnetite. Rather than preserving its original crystallization signature, magnetite records a complex history of hydrothermal activity, including Cu-Au-PGE-bearing magmatic-hydrothermal fluid circulation and repeated, superimposed coupled dissolution-reprecipitation events. These findings align with those from other magmatic-hydrothermal ore systems such as iron oxide copper-gold and Kiruna-type iron oxide-apatite deposits, emphasizing the widespread nature of magnetite reequilibration and the need for caution when using magnetite as a petrogenetic indicator.

item.title: Linking Himalayan uplift to crustal deformation: Structural and multi-chronometer constraints from the Kangmar dome
item.dc:creator: Zhen Li
Pengfei Li
Dewen Zheng
Fred Jourdan
Gideon Rosenbaum
Qiang Wang
item.description: The highest topography on Earth occurs in the Himalayan orogen, which is the quintessential example of continental collision. To elucidate the crustal processes that accompanied the rise of the Himalayas, we investigated the deformational history of the Kangmar dome in the eastern Himalayas. By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history. Published: 2026-07-05T21:54:24+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.587 (consider)

Authors: Zhen Li; Pengfei Li; Dewen Zheng; Fred Jourdan; Gideon Rosenbaum; Qiang Wang

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105616

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

The highest topography on Earth occurs in the Himalayan orogen, which is the quintessential example of continental collision. To elucidate the crustal processes that accompanied the rise of the Himalayas, we investigated the deformational history of the Kangmar dome in the eastern Himalayas. By integrating field mapping with monazite U-Th-Pb, mica 40Ar/39Ar, and zircon and apatite (UTh)/He geochronology, we identified three distinct phases of deformation. The first phase involved north-south contractional deformation at ca. 51–44 Ma. This was followed by pervasive top-to-north ductile shearing at ca. 26–17 Ma, which exhumed deep-crustal rocks. North-south shortening was responsible for the final doming, at ca. 15–8 Ma, which involved two distinct cooling and exhumation stages: an earlier bottom-up cooling event that was driven by underthrusting of the Tethyan Himalayan Sequence below the Kangmar dome, and a later pop-up exhumation of the dome core. These deformation phases, which were driven by India-Asia convergence, are correlated with structural patterns across the Himalayan orogen. The deformation record is temporally linked to the episodic uplift history of the Himalayas, thus elucidating potential driving mechanisms. Early Eocene contraction was likely responsible for elevating the Himalayas above sea level. The main rise of the Himalayas, which occurred during the Early Miocene, was possibly triggered by the southward migration of deep-crustal rocks toward the southern Himalayan front. Collectively, the deformation history of the Kangmar dome provides critical insights into the tectonic evolution of this collisional orogen, including its uplift history.

item.title: Neoproterozoic lamprophyre from the Southern Granulite Terrane, India: Implications for calc-alkaline magmatism in an aborted rift setting
item.dc:creator: M.R. Nandu
Y. Anilkumar
M.P. Manu Prasanth
Cheng-Xue Yang
M. Santosh
Sanghoon Kwon
Sung Won Kim
A.P. Pradeepkumar
Yoshiyuki Iizuka
S. Anbazhagan
Tamilarasan Kuppusamy
item.description: Calc-alkaline lamprophyres, owing to their distinctive geochemical signature, serve as crucial probes for deciphering continental-scale geodynamic processes as well as the tectonomagmatic evolution of the continental lithosphere. Here we present petrological, mineral chemical, whole-rock geochemical, and U-Pb zircon geochronological data on the calc-alkaline lamprophyre (spessartite) from the Salem Mafic–Ultramafic Complex (SMUC) of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT), India. The Salem lamprophyre exhibits a characteristic porphyritic to panidiomorphic texture dominated by amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) phenocrysts. Clinopyroxene (diopside) occurs as microphenocrysts, while feldspar (albite) is largely confined to the groundmass. The high Mg# values (80–83) and elevated concentrations of compatible trace elements (Cr and Ni) indicate the primitive nature of the melt, whereas the incompatible trace element ratios exclude any significant crustal contamination and closely resemble those of subduction-related calc-alkaline lamprophyres reported globally. Elevated Ba/Nb and low Nb/La ratios indicate derivation from an enriched lithospheric mantle source. The pronounced negative Ti–Nb–Ta and Zr-Hf anomalies, together with diagnostic geochemical ratios (e.g., Hf/Sm, Ta/La, Nb/Yb, La/Nb, Ba/Nb), suggest that the mantle source was metasomatized predominantly by dehydrated fluids released from a subducting slab. Zircon grains from the Salem lamprophyre exhibit well-developed magmatic crystallization features, and LA–ICP–MS U-Pb analyses yield a weighted mean age of 805 ± 2.7 Ma (MSWD = 0.54), which is comparable with the Tonian (ca. 830–720 Ma) alkaline–carbonatite magmatism in the region. We envisage that the Neoarchean subduction-modified lithospheric mantle was reactivated and partially melted during Neoproterozoic extension to generate the hydrous parental magma of the Salem calc-alkaline lamprophyre. Published: 2026-07-10T15:36:16+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.587 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: M.R. Nandu; Y. Anilkumar; M.P. Manu Prasanth; Cheng-Xue Yang; M. Santosh; Sanghoon Kwon; Sung Won Kim; A.P. Pradeepkumar; Yoshiyuki Iizuka; S. Anbazhagan; Tamilarasan Kuppusamy

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108710

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

Calc-alkaline lamprophyres, owing to their distinctive geochemical signature, serve as crucial probes for deciphering continental-scale geodynamic processes as well as the tectonomagmatic evolution of the continental lithosphere. Here we present petrological, mineral chemical, whole-rock geochemical, and U-Pb zircon geochronological data on the calc-alkaline lamprophyre (spessartite) from the Salem Mafic–Ultramafic Complex (SMUC) of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT), India. The Salem lamprophyre exhibits a characteristic porphyritic to panidiomorphic texture dominated by amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) phenocrysts. Clinopyroxene (diopside) occurs as microphenocrysts, while feldspar (albite) is largely confined to the groundmass. The high Mg# values (80–83) and elevated concentrations of compatible trace elements (Cr and Ni) indicate the primitive nature of the melt, whereas the incompatible trace element ratios exclude any significant crustal contamination and closely resemble those of subduction-related calc-alkaline lamprophyres reported globally. Elevated Ba/Nb and low Nb/La ratios indicate derivation from an enriched lithospheric mantle source. The pronounced negative Ti–Nb–Ta and Zr-Hf anomalies, together with diagnostic geochemical ratios (e.g., Hf/Sm, Ta/La, Nb/Yb, La/Nb, Ba/Nb), suggest that the mantle source was metasomatized predominantly by dehydrated fluids released from a subducting slab. Zircon grains from the Salem lamprophyre exhibit well-developed magmatic crystallization features, and LA–ICP–MS U-Pb analyses yield a weighted mean age of 805 ± 2.7 Ma (MSWD = 0.54), which is comparable with the Tonian (ca. 830–720 Ma) alkaline–carbonatite magmatism in the region. We envisage that the Neoarchean subduction-modified lithospheric mantle was reactivated and partially melted during Neoproterozoic extension to generate the hydrous parental magma of the Salem calc-alkaline lamprophyre.

item.title: Is Hg recycling into the Earth’s mantle negligible?
item.dc:creator: F. Narduzzi
S. Covelli
M. Pistone
I. Pitcairn
R. Tribuzio
L. Ziberna
item.description: Mercury (Hg), with its ultra-trace abundance in rocks and high volatility, challenges the investigation of its distribution and mobilisation in geological systems. Based on similar Hg isotope mass independent fractionation (i.e. Δ199Hg) values between marine and terrestrial sediments and magmatic rocks, it is commonly suggested that Hg has been continuously exchanged between the Earth’s mantle and the atmosphere through subduction recycling. However, a review of Hg concentrations in ophiolites and orogenic peridotites, mantle xenoliths, mantle derived basalts and mafic rocks (Mg# ≥60), and variably metamorphosed collision and subduction related rocks suggests otherwise. Ultra-trace Hg concentrations in mantle rocks and mantle derived magmas are inconsistent with Hg recycling into the mantle. In fact, collision and subduction related rocks progressively lose Hg with increasing metamorphic grade, indicating that only ≤1 ng/g of Hg is transferred to the sub-arc mantle melting regions. These observations also suggest that Hg recycling was inhibited during the Archean. Here we warn about the risk of using Hg isotopes alone to interpret the Hg cycle on Earth and urge the need for new and accurate Hg concentrations in crystalline rocks from different geological settings. Published: 2026-07-10T08:37:17+00:00 Venue: Geochemical Perspectives Letters Score: 0.586 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: F. Narduzzi; S. Covelli; M. Pistone; I. Pitcairn; R. Tribuzio; L. Ziberna

Journal: Geochemical Perspectives Letters

DOI: 10.7185/geochemlet.2626

Relevance: 0.586 (consider)

Mercury (Hg), with its ultra-trace abundance in rocks and high volatility, challenges the investigation of its distribution and mobilisation in geological systems. Based on similar Hg isotope mass independent fractionation (i.e. Δ199Hg) values between marine and terrestrial sediments and magmatic rocks, it is commonly suggested that Hg has been continuously exchanged between the Earth’s mantle and the atmosphere through subduction recycling. However, a review of Hg concentrations in ophiolites and orogenic peridotites, mantle xenoliths, mantle derived basalts and mafic rocks (Mg# ≥60), and variably metamorphosed collision and subduction related rocks suggests otherwise. Ultra-trace Hg concentrations in mantle rocks and mantle derived magmas are inconsistent with Hg recycling into the mantle. In fact, collision and subduction related rocks progressively lose Hg with increasing metamorphic grade, indicating that only ≤1 ng/g of Hg is transferred to the sub-arc mantle melting regions. These observations also suggest that Hg recycling was inhibited during the Archean. Here we warn about the risk of using Hg isotopes alone to interpret the Hg cycle on Earth and urge the need for new and accurate Hg concentrations in crystalline rocks from different geological settings.

item.title: Fingerprints of s-cl-bearing fluids deduced from metal and mineral microinclusions in Quaternary Kamchatka lavas
item.dc:creator: Pavel Kepezhinskas
Nikolai Berdnikov
Nadezhda Potapova
Valeria Krutikova
item.description: Quaternary ankaramite, basalt, andesite and dacite from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes in Kamchatka contain numerous mineral microinclusions in rock-forming silicate and accessory oxide minerals as well as in groundmass volcanic glass. We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. S-Cl-bearing microinclusions as Ag (± Cu) chlorides, composite S-O-C-bearing metal chlorides, Ag (±Cu) sulfides, sulfides of Fe, Ni, Cu, Pb, Zn and Sb as well as barite, cassiterite and apatite are very characteristic for these rocks. Primary-textured, magmatic microinclusions in olivine, pyroxenes, amphibole, plagioclase and fresh volcanic glass show no evidence for the later-stage transformations. Secondary-textured, post-magmatic microinclusions are localized in pores, voids and fractures or along the boundaries of silicate minerals and in association with secondary minerals. Magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing microinclusion assemblages almost completely overlap compositionally suggesting that S-Cl-bearing fluids accompanied formation of Kamchatka lavas from magmatic differentiation in sub-volcanic conduits to their emplacement, cooling and degassing above the surface as well as possible post-eruptive transformations near active fumarolic fields. Lavas from the Avacha, Bakening, Mutnovsky and Gorely volcanoes display elevated Pb, Zn, Sb, Cu and Ag concentrations in comparison with the average MORB and upper continental crust. Some volcanic samples with both magmatic and post-magmatic S-Cl-bearing mineral microinclusions have higher Mo, Sn, Sb and Ag contents than previously reported for Kamchatka volcanoes suggesting that sulfur and chlorine potentially contribute to metal enrichment in magmas and associated fluids and facilitate transport of certain ore metals in the Kamchatka subduction zone. Published: 2026-07-04T15:23:16+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.586 (consider)

We have studied the composition and textural setting of these microinclusions along with the petrology and geochemictsry of their host lavas using optical petrography, major and trace element geochemistry and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS). The microinclusions are represented by Au and Au-bearing alloys, Ag and Ag-bearing alloys, partially oxidized siderophile (W) and chalcophile (Pb, Zn, Cu, Bi, Sb, Pb) metals. S-Cl-bearing microinclusions as Ag (± Cu) chlorides, composite S-O-C-bearing metal chlorides, Ag (±Cu) sulfides, sulfides of Fe, Ni, Cu, Pb, Zn and Sb as well as barite, cassiterite and apatite are very characteristic for these rocks. item.title: Calcium Solubility of Bridgmanite in Subducted Basalt in Earth's Lower Mantle
item.dc:creator: Chengwei Zhang
Takashi Yoshino
Elias El Ghazaoui
Stella Chariton
Vitali B. Prakapenka
Luo Li
Yanyao Zhang
Jung‐Fu Lin
item.description: Abstract The calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the resulting abundance of davemaoite under lower‐mantle pressure‐temperature conditions have been under debate following a recent report of extensive calcium dissolution in bridgmanite. We experimentally investigated the calcium solubility in bridgmanite in a basaltic composition at pressures up to 125 GPa and temperatures up to 3,520 K. Our experimental data are modeled thermodynamically along the bridgmanite‐davemaoite solvus to quantify the calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the davemaoite proportion in basaltic and pyrolitic assemblages along lower mantle geotherms. Our results indicate that bridgmanite hosts only 0.001–0.03 Ca cations per formula unit in the lower mantle, while davemaoite remains as an abundant mineral, accounting for 8 and 25 vol% of pyrolitic and basaltic mineralogical models, respectively, along representative lower‐mantle geotherms. Our thermoelastic modeling further indicates that the effect of calcium dissolution on the density and bulk sound velocity of lower‐mantle assemblages is negligible. Published: 2026-07-10T13:21:22+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.585 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chengwei Zhang; Takashi Yoshino; Elias El Ghazaoui; Stella Chariton; Vitali B. Prakapenka; Luo Li; Yanyao Zhang; Jung‐Fu Lin

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2026gl121981

Relevance: 0.585 (consider)

Abstract The calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the resulting abundance of davemaoite under lower‐mantle pressure‐temperature conditions have been under debate following a recent report of extensive calcium dissolution in bridgmanite. We experimentally investigated the calcium solubility in bridgmanite in a basaltic composition at pressures up to 125 GPa and temperatures up to 3,520 K. Our experimental data are modeled thermodynamically along the bridgmanite‐davemaoite solvus to quantify the calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the davemaoite proportion in basaltic and pyrolitic assemblages along lower mantle geotherms. Our results indicate that bridgmanite hosts only 0.001–0.03 Ca cations per formula unit in the lower mantle, while davemaoite remains as an abundant mineral, accounting for 8 and 25 vol% of pyrolitic and basaltic mineralogical models, respectively, along representative lower‐mantle geotherms. Our thermoelastic modeling further indicates that the effect of calcium dissolution on the density and bulk sound velocity of lower‐mantle assemblages is negligible.

item.title: Vanadium isotopes demonstrate depth-dependent magma differentiation in Rindjani Volcano, Sunda arc
item.dc:creator: Xin Ding
Min Ji
Weixin Lv
Fang Huang
Erik V. Sørensen
Paul M. Holm
John D. Foden
item.description: The andesitic composition of the continental crust—characterized by high SiO2 and low FeOT contents—has been attributed to differentiation processes operating in arc settings. One contributing mechanism could be amphibole-forming peritectic reaction at the crust–mantle boundary; however, its role in arc magma differentiation remains poorly constrained. Here we present high-precision δ 51/50V data (hereafter δ51V) for volcanic rocks from the Rindjani suite in the Sunda arc that span compositions from high-Al basalts to trachydacites, investigating how V isotopes respond to amphibole-controlled magma differentiation. At the crust–mantle boundary, amphibole-forming peritectic reactions transform H2O-rich magma into Si-rich melt and induce minor δ51V variation of approximately 0.08‰. H2O-rich magmas that bypass the peritectic reaction ascend to the shallow crust and differentiate from high-Al basalts to high-K andesites, showing limited δ51V variation from −0.76‰ to −0.66‰. Shallow-level differentiation of the Si-rich melt from andesites to trachydacites produces a sharp increase in δ51V from −0.62‰ to −0.04‰, consistent with an average mineral-melt fractionation factor Δ51Vmineral–melt ≈ −0.30‰. Published Fe−Ti isotope data show that δ56Fe remained constant, whereas δ49Ti responded to differentiation throughout the compositional range of the Rindjani suite. The decoupled Fe–Ti–V isotope systematics reveal differences in elemental mass balance and mineral–melt isotope fractionation during magma differentiation. These results show that amphibole-forming peritectic reactions fundamentally modify melt chemistry, thereby enabling significant V isotope fractionation associated with magnetite crystallization at shallow levels. Our findings highlight the depth–dependent differentiation framework of arc magmas and advance understanding of the V isotopic pathways involved in continental crust formation. Published: 2026-07-05T21:55:12+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.584 (consider)

Our findings highlight the depth–dependent differentiation framework of arc magmas and advance understanding of the V isotopic pathways involved in continental crust formation.

item.title: Sequential serpentinization reactions revealed by boron isotope systematics of the Lost City vent fluids
item.dc:creator: Aled D. Evans
Heather J. Goring-Harford
Rosalind M. Coggon
Rachael H. James
Caitlin McCarthy
Matthew J. Cooper
Gavin L. Foster
Gretchen L. Früh-Green
Susan Q. Lang
Damon A.H. Teagle
item.description: Hydrothermal vent fluids reflect interactions between oceanic lithosphere and seawater, but to date, the processes resulting in unusual hyperalkaline (up to pH 11) fluids venting ultramafic-hosted systems, such as the Lost City Hydrothermal Field, remain enigmatic. To elucidate fluid reaction pathways, we present boron (B) concentration and isotope analyses from fluids venting the Lost City, located off axis atop the serpentinized Atlantis Massif just west of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge at 30°N. Our data reveal that fluid B concentrations are markedly lower and isotopically lighter than that of seawater. These data contrast with the accepted understanding of B isotope systematics that predicts that the progressive removal of B from seawater during serpentine formation should result in low-B fluids with δ11B values higher than that of seawater. We utilize a mechanistic B isotope fractionation model coupled with a Bayesian inversion approach to robustly evaluate fluid-rock interactions and propose that Lost City fluid B compositions reflect a two-step process. Initially, B is progressively removed from seawater during serpentine formation, resulting in the generation of an effectively B-free serpentinizing fluid. Subsequently, B remobilized from previously formed serpentine is transferred to later fluids, resulting in the low-δ11B end-member values analyzed. Such an evolution demonstrates that fluid-rock interactions both add fluid-mobile elements such as B to mantle peridotites as well as remobilize them. Thus, analyzed serpentine δ11B signatures reflect the cumulative impact of B-consuming serpentinization and later B-remobilizing serpentinite recrystallization. Consequently, serpentinite alteration, in addition to serpentinization hydration reactions, actively contributes to chemical exchange between the mantle and the oceans. Published: 2026-07-10T21:19:01+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.584 (consider)

Authors: Aled D. Evans; Heather J. Goring-Harford; Rosalind M. Coggon; Rachael H. James; Caitlin McCarthy; Matthew J. Cooper; Gavin L. Foster; Gretchen L. Früh-Green; Susan Q. Lang; Damon A.H. Teagle

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54880.1

Relevance: 0.584 (consider)

Hydrothermal vent fluids reflect interactions between oceanic lithosphere and seawater, but to date, the processes resulting in unusual hyperalkaline (up to pH 11) fluids venting ultramafic-hosted systems, such as the Lost City Hydrothermal Field, remain enigmatic. To elucidate fluid reaction pathways, we present boron (B) concentration and isotope analyses from fluids venting the Lost City, located off axis atop the serpentinized Atlantis Massif just west of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge at 30°N. Our data reveal that fluid B concentrations are markedly lower and isotopically lighter than that of seawater. These data contrast with the accepted understanding of B isotope systematics that predicts that the progressive removal of B from seawater during serpentine formation should result in low-B fluids with δ11B values higher than that of seawater. We utilize a mechanistic B isotope fractionation model coupled with a Bayesian inversion approach to robustly evaluate fluid-rock interactions and propose that Lost City fluid B compositions reflect a two-step process. Initially, B is progressively removed from seawater during serpentine formation, resulting in the generation of an effectively B-free serpentinizing fluid. Subsequently, B remobilized from previously formed serpentine is transferred to later fluids, resulting in the low-δ11B end-member values analyzed. Such an evolution demonstrates that fluid-rock interactions both add fluid-mobile elements such as B to mantle peridotites as well as remobilize them. Thus, analyzed serpentine δ11B signatures reflect the cumulative impact of B-consuming serpentinization and later B-remobilizing serpentinite recrystallization. Consequently, serpentinite alteration, in addition to serpentinization hydration reactions, actively contributes to chemical exchange between the mantle and the oceans.

item.title: Devonian Jinshuikou pegmatites at the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau: UPb and SmNd constraints on their genetic relationship with regional granitoids
item.dc:creator: Si-Qi Liu
Shao-Yong Jiang
Mostafa Fayek
Hui-Min Su
Yu-Ying Che
Wei Wang
Qinglin Xia
item.description: Granitic pegmatites represent critical repositories of strategic rare metals (e.g., Li, Be, Nb, Ta) and are conventionally interpreted as terminal products of highly evolved granitic magmas. However, this classic genetic model was frequently challenged where pronounced age gaps exist between pegmatites and their putative host granitoids. Along the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau lies a world-class lithium metallogenic belt hosting approximately 14.8 million tons of Li2O resources. As a key segment of this belt, the East Kunlun Orogen contains widespread granitoids and newly recognized pegmatite dikes in the Jinshuikou area, yet their emplacement ages and genetic linkage between each other remain poorly constrained. Here we present integrated in situ UPb geochronology, mineral chemistry, and SmNd isotope data for monazite, garnet, and columbite group minerals (CGMs) from the pegmatites to resolve these issues. UPb dating of monazite and CGMs yields consistent ages of 372–375 Ma, firmly establishing a Devonian crystallization age for the pegmatites. Despite a ~ 15 Myr age difference relative to the youngest coeval Devonian granitoids (ca. 389 Ma), SmNd isotopic systematics strongly support a genetic connection between them. Monazite from pegmatites yields a SmNd isochron age of 390.2 ± 6.0 Ma and εNd(t) values (−6.01 to −5.39) identical to those of coeval I-type granitoids in the region. These data indicate that the pegmatites and associated granitoids originated from a common deep crustal magma reservoir generated by partial melting of ancient continental crust with limited input from juvenile mantle materials. The pegmatites represent the final products of protracted magmatic evolution within a long-lived crustal magma chamber. This study not only documents a previously overlooked Paleozoic rare-metal pegmatite system in the East Kunlun Orogen but also demonstrates that age gaps alone do not negate a cogenetic relationship between pegmatites and regional granitoids, with important implications for rare-metal exploration models in orogenic belts. Published: 2026-07-06T23:28:42+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.582 (consider)
