Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108707

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

Granitic pegmatites represent critical repositories of strategic rare metals (e.g., Li, Be, Nb, Ta) and are conventionally interpreted as terminal products of highly evolved granitic magmas. However, this classic genetic model was frequently challenged where pronounced age gaps exist between pegmatites and their putative host granitoids. Along the northern margin of the Tibetan Plateau lies a world-class lithium metallogenic belt hosting approximately 14.8 million tons of Li2O resources. As a key segment of this belt, the East Kunlun Orogen contains widespread granitoids and newly recognized pegmatite dikes in the Jinshuikou area, yet their emplacement ages and genetic linkage between each other remain poorly constrained. Here we present integrated in situ UPb geochronology, mineral chemistry, and SmNd isotope data for monazite, garnet, and columbite group minerals (CGMs) from the pegmatites to resolve these issues. UPb dating of monazite and CGMs yields consistent ages of 372–375 Ma, firmly establishing a Devonian crystallization age for the pegmatites. Despite a ~ 15 Myr age difference relative to the youngest coeval Devonian granitoids (ca. 389 Ma), SmNd isotopic systematics strongly support a genetic connection between them. Monazite from pegmatites yields a SmNd isochron age of 390.2 ± 6.0 Ma and εNd(t) values (−6.01 to −5.39) identical to those of coeval I-type granitoids in the region. These data indicate that the pegmatites and associated granitoids originated from a common deep crustal magma reservoir generated by partial melting of ancient continental crust with limited input from juvenile mantle materials. The pegmatites represent the final products of protracted magmatic evolution within a long-lived crustal magma chamber. This study not only documents a previously overlooked Paleozoic rare-metal pegmatite system in the East Kunlun Orogen but also demonstrates that age gaps alone do not negate a cogenetic relationship between pegmatites and regional granitoids, with important implications for rare-metal exploration models in orogenic belts.

item.title: Reassessing long-term exhumation rates in magmatic terranes
item.dc:creator: F.J. Cooper
B.A. Adams
S.I.R. Dahlström
T.A. Ehlers
M.C. van Soest
K.V. Hodges
B.R. Jicha
B.S. Singer
J. Cortes Yañez
R.J. Perkins
item.description: Crustal exhumation is central to mountain building, weathering, and sediment production processes, which significantly influence the composition and behavior of Earth’s oceans and atmosphere. It also controls the formation, enrichment, and preservation of porphyry copper deposits, which are a vital source of metals for the clean energy technologies underpinning the global green energy transition. Exhumation rates are typically determined from rock cooling histories combined with a geothermal model. However, in magmatically active regions, models can overpredict exhumation rates because the elevated heat signature is mistakenly attributed to advection driven by rapid exhumation. We present a method to accurately constrain exhumation histories in magmatic terranes that combines thermokinematic modeling of thermochronometric cooling histories with independent geobarometric constraints on the total exhumed crustal thickness. Focusing on the Central Andes, which has a prolonged history of magmatic and magmatic-hydrothermal activity, we find that accounting for this additional source of heat results in exhumed crustal thicknesses within error of the independent constraints, whereas ignoring it results in exhumed thicknesses up to four times greater than geologically plausible. Published: 2026-07-07T04:39:43+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: F.J. Cooper; B.A. Adams; S.I.R. Dahlström; T.A. Ehlers; M.C. van Soest; K.V. Hodges; B.R. Jicha; B.S. Singer; J. Cortes Yañez; R.J. Perkins

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120217

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

Crustal exhumation is central to mountain building, weathering, and sediment production processes, which significantly influence the composition and behavior of Earth’s oceans and atmosphere. It also controls the formation, enrichment, and preservation of porphyry copper deposits, which are a vital source of metals for the clean energy technologies underpinning the global green energy transition. Exhumation rates are typically determined from rock cooling histories combined with a geothermal model. However, in magmatically active regions, models can overpredict exhumation rates because the elevated heat signature is mistakenly attributed to advection driven by rapid exhumation. We present a method to accurately constrain exhumation histories in magmatic terranes that combines thermokinematic modeling of thermochronometric cooling histories with independent geobarometric constraints on the total exhumed crustal thickness. Focusing on the Central Andes, which has a prolonged history of magmatic and magmatic-hydrothermal activity, we find that accounting for this additional source of heat results in exhumed crustal thicknesses within error of the independent constraints, whereas ignoring it results in exhumed thicknesses up to four times greater than geologically plausible.

item.title: Mobilization of insoluble Fe(III) and phosphate in sulfate- and carbonate-rich fluids
item.dc:creator: Huan Chen
Richen Zhong
Yuling Xie
Xinfu Zhao
Joël Brugger
Hao Cui
Chang Yu
Mojie Li
item.description: Iron(III) oxides and phosphates are considered to be insoluble in geofluids but are common products of hydrothermal mineralization. We investigated the capability of sulfate- and/or carbonate-rich fluids to dissolve hematite and apatite using a hydrothermal diamond anvil cell (HDAC). We found that substantial Fe(III) can be dissolved in these fluids, challenging the view that hydrothermal Fe transport requires the predominance of Fe(II). This provides insights into the genetic link between sulfate-rich lithologies (e.g., evaporites and carbonatites) and Fe mineralization in iron oxide-apatite (IOA), iron oxide copper-gold (IOCG), and carbonatite-related deposits. In addition, the co-mobility of phosphate and Fe(III) in these fluids not only explains their coexistence in a series of deposit types, but also implies that some conventionally immobile components (e.g., high field strength elements) may be mobile in sulfate- or carbonate-rich “unconventional” fluids. Published: 2026-07-09T20:08:57+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Huan Chen; Richen Zhong; Yuling Xie; Xinfu Zhao; Joël Brugger; Hao Cui; Chang Yu; Mojie Li

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54368.1

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

Iron(III) oxides and phosphates are considered to be insoluble in geofluids but are common products of hydrothermal mineralization. We investigated the capability of sulfate- and/or carbonate-rich fluids to dissolve hematite and apatite using a hydrothermal diamond anvil cell (HDAC). We found that substantial Fe(III) can be dissolved in these fluids, challenging the view that hydrothermal Fe transport requires the predominance of Fe(II). This provides insights into the genetic link between sulfate-rich lithologies (e.g., evaporites and carbonatites) and Fe mineralization in iron oxide-apatite (IOA), iron oxide copper-gold (IOCG), and carbonatite-related deposits. In addition, the co-mobility of phosphate and Fe(III) in these fluids not only explains their coexistence in a series of deposit types, but also implies that some conventionally immobile components (e.g., high field strength elements) may be mobile in sulfate- or carbonate-rich “unconventional” fluids.

item.title: Structure, composition and dynamics of divergent and convergent plate margins
item.dc:creator: Yong-Fei Zheng
Yi-Xiang Chen
Li-Qun Dai
Wei Dan
Sanzhong Li
item.description: Plate margins are the dynamic boundaries where lithospheric plates interact, driving Earth’s tectonic evolution. While the asthenospheric material and energy are transported along divergent plate margins to the Earth’s surface, the surface material is transferred along convergent plate margins into the Earth’s interior. Therefore, plate divergence and convergence are two key mechanisms for the exchange of material and energy between the lithosphere and asthenosphere. A great deal of advances in understanding thestructure, composition and dynamics of divergent and convergent plate margins have been made in the past two decades. Divergent margins are manifested as seafloor spreading and continental rift zones, and they occur as mid-ocean ridges, successful and failed continental rifts in different phases. Convergent margins are manifested as oceanic subduction and continental collision zones, and their formation and evolution are dictated by the properties of converging plates. Therefore, they record a series of processes for the Wilson cycle in plate tectonics. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes of plate margins are two critical variables in modulating the internal and external Earth systems. In general, the geothermal gradient of plate margins changes from cold through warm to hot during the transition from convergence to divergence, and it correlates with the dynamic regime of either horizontal or vertical forces via specific tectonic processes. Cold subduction proceeds at low geothermal gradients, producing low T/P Alpine-type blueschist to eclogite facies metamorphic rocks; warm collision proceeds at mediate geothermal gradients, leading to medium T/P Barrovian-type amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks; and hot rifting proceeds at high geothermal gradients, generating high T/P amphibolite to granulite facies metamorphic rocks. Oceanic plates may subduct either actively or passively beneath continental margins, giving rise to different products at the overriding continental margins. Active subduction results in accretionary wedge, andesitic arc magmatism and porphyry mineralization, which are absent in the case of passive subduction. Continental assembly progresses from soft collision through hard collision to deep subduction, producing different types of regional metamorphism along convergent plate margins. Syn-collisional orogeny during continental assembly is characterized by medium T/P Barrovian-type metamorphism in the compressional regime, whereas post-collisional orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. Such spatiotemporal relationships between the dynamic and thermobaric regimes are definite at plate margins. Magmatism always proceeds at high geothermal gradients and thus is associated with extensional regimes either in the late stage of oceanic subduction or in the post-collisional stage. In this regard, caution must be exercised in linking HT/LP metamorphism and felsic magmatism to continental collision and supercontinental assembly. The formation and evolution of intracontinental tectonics have the inheritance and development relationships to the structure and composition of precursor plate margins. The thermobaric and dynamic regimes are two key variables in dictating the physicochemical properties and geodynamic processes of continental interiors. Thethermo-mechanical coupling is significant at the plate margins, where feedback between thermobaric regimes (i.e., geothermal gradients) and dynamic forces (i.e., buoyancy vs gravity, and compression vs extension) governs a series of geological processes such as deformation, metamorphism and magmatism. Therefore, plate tectonics operate viafeedback loopswhere thermobaric anomalies (e.g., asthenospheric heating vs slab cooling) modify rock rheology, inducing these geological processes that reshapes the architecture of active and fossil plate margins. As a result, continental rifting along previously convergent plate margins plays a critical role not only in physical and chemical reworking of the plate margins but also in initiating new Wilson cycles. As such, intracontinental orogeny during continental rifting is featured by high T/P Buchan-type metamorphism in the extensional regime. The relevant elements are the important components of plate tectonics theory and thus have significant linkages to resources, energy and disasters. Published: 2026-07-09T12:55:31+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.576 (consider)

item.title: Subduction Initiation and Early Subduction Zone Development: Insights From the P–T–t Path of the Bay of Islands Metamorphic Sole
item.dc:creator: François Fournier‐Roy
Carl Guilmette
Mark Coleman
Kyle Larson
Douglas K. Tinkham
Alana M. Hinchey
John Wakabayashi
Jean H. Bédard
Louise Mérit
Philippe Agard
Loïc Labrousse
Antoine Godet
item.description: ABSTRACT The processes involved during subduction initiation (SI) remain poorly constrained, in part due to the rarity of geological archives. Ophiolites and their underlying metamorphic soles are thought to form during subduction zone infancy and may thus provide critical constraints. Metamorphic soles are derived from oceanic crust of the lower plate accreted to the base of the upper plate following SI and are exhumed during upper plate extension and ophiolitic crust generation. Their metamorphic evolution therefore provides insights into the thermal conditions at the nascent subduction plate interface and the trajectory of the lower plate during subduction infancy. In this study, we revisit the Bay of Islands complex (BOIC) metamorphic sole in the Newfoundland Appalachian Orogen and constrain P–T–t paths using phase equilibria modelling, melt reintegration and in situ U–Pb titanite geochronology. Our findings reveal generic SI and early subduction processes that align with geochronologic and metamorphic constraints reported for other soles as well as with the proposed early development of some modern subduction zones. The BOIC sole exhibits an inverted metamorphic gradient, with granulite and amphibolite facies metabasites at upper and middle structural levels, and greenschist facies metasedimentary and meta‐igneous rocks in the lowest level. Our results are consistent with burial of garnet–clinopyroxene–amphibolite sole rocks to peak pressure conditions of ~14 kbar and 750°C–850°C, followed by decompression to peak temperature conditions of 880°C–940°C and 10.5–12 kbar. The immediately underlying plagioclase–hornblende amphibolites equilibrated at ~740°C and ~8.5 kbar. Sole metamorphism produced melts and fluids that may have contributed to arc magmatism during early subduction. Titanite geochronology of granulite facies samples yields dates of 512 ± 11 Ma and 501 ± 11 Ma, interpreted to constrain prograde metamorphism. Our results indicate that the sole formed along a clockwise P–T–t path, with initial burial occurring along a relatively cold 18°C/km metamorphic gradient, followed by heating of the subduction interface during decompression and eventually exhumation to a 26°C–27°C/km gradient. Peak temperature conditions in the sole were coeval with c . 484–488 Ma ophiolitic crust formation. We propose that subduction initiated from far‐field forces and evolved to a self‐sustained state as the slab pull force increased. Upwelling of hot asthenospheric mantle during rollback can explain decompressional heating and ensuing exhumation along an increasing thermal gradient, coeval with upper plate extension. Published: 2026-07-07T12:16:02+00:00 Venue: Journal of Metamorphic Geology Score: 0.575 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: François Fournier‐Roy; Carl Guilmette; Mark Coleman; Kyle Larson; Douglas K. Tinkham; Alana M. Hinchey; John Wakabayashi; Jean H. Bédard; Louise Mérit; Philippe Agard; Loïc Labrousse; Antoine Godet

Journal: Journal of Metamorphic Geology

DOI: 10.1111/jmg.70045

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

ABSTRACT The processes involved during subduction initiation (SI) remain poorly constrained, in part due to the rarity of geological archives. Ophiolites and their underlying metamorphic soles are thought to form during subduction zone infancy and may thus provide critical constraints. Their metamorphic evolution therefore provides insights into the thermal conditions at the nascent subduction plate interface and the trajectory of the lower plate during subduction infancy. In this study, we revisit the Bay of Islands complex (BOIC) metamorphic sole in the Newfoundland Appalachian Orogen and constrain P–T–t paths using phase equilibria modelling, melt reintegration and in situ U–Pb titanite geochronology. Our findings reveal generic SI and early subduction processes that align with geochronologic and metamorphic constraints reported for other soles as well as with the proposed early development of some modern subduction zones. The BOIC sole exhibits an inverted metamorphic gradient, with granulite and amphibolite facies metabasites at upper and middle structural levels, and greenschist facies metasedimentary and meta‐igneous rocks in the lowest level. Our results are consistent with burial of garnet–clinopyroxene–amphibolite sole rocks to peak pressure conditions of ~14 kbar and 750°C–850°C, followed by decompression to peak temperature conditions of 880°C–940°C and 10.5–12 kbar. The immediately underlying plagioclase–hornblende amphibolites equilibrated at ~740°C and ~8.5 kbar. Sole metamorphism produced melts and fluids that may have contributed to arc magmatism during early subduction. Titanite geochronology of granulite facies samples yields dates of 512 ± 11 Ma and 501 ± 11 Ma, interpreted to constrain prograde metamorphism. Our results indicate that the sole formed along a clockwise P–T–t path, with initial burial occurring along a relatively cold 18°C/km metamorphic gradient, followed by heating of the subduction interface during decompression and eventually exhumation to a 26°C–27°C/km gradient. Peak temperature conditions in the sole were coeval with c . 484–488 Ma ophiolitic crust formation. We propose that subduction initiated from far‐field forces and evolved to a self‐sustained state as the slab pull force increased. Upwelling of hot asthenospheric mantle during rollback can explain decompressional heating and ensuing exhumation along an increasing thermal gradient, coeval with upper plate extension.

item.title: Tracing hydrothermal fluid evolution of the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit (Eastern China) using integrated infrared spectroscopic characteristics and magnetic susceptibility
item.dc:creator: Shili Peng
Shitao Zhang
Hao Xu
Gaobin Chu
Longfei He
Yu Zhang
Hua Zhang
Rucao Li
Yihui Lyu
Di Ding
Siyuan Zhang
Haisen Huang
item.description: The Middle–Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt (MLYRB) is one of the dominant skarn–porphyry Cu–Au metallogenic belts in Eastern China. Within this belt, skarn–porphyry Cu–Au deposits show a close genetic linkage to Late Mesozoic high‑K calc‑alkaline granitoids, and are associated with variable degrees of critical metals mineralization (i.e., Co, Te, Se and Re). Previous studies have generally suggested that these granitoids possess limited W metallogenic potential; nevertheless, several concealed medium‑to‑large W–Cu–Mo deposits have been discovered in their peripheral areas in recent years. At present, the hydrothermal fluid evolution of these W‑rich skarn–porphyry Cu polymetallic deposits remains poorly constrained, and their deep exploration indicators still need further optimization. This study focuses on the Tongshankou skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposit in the westernmost segment of the MLYRB. The deposit hosts contained metal resources of 0.55 Mt Cu, 0.01 Mt Mo, and 0.012 Mt WO3, with ore grades of 0.86 wt%, 0.10 wt%, and 0.19 wt%, respectively. By combining detailed petrographic observations with short-wave infrared (SWIR) and thermal infrared (TIR) spectral alteration mapping, this study pinpoints the precise ore-forming stages and delineates detailed alteration zoning features in the Tongshankou Cu–Mo–W deposit. Building on SWIR and TIR spectral parameters, alongside volumetric bulk magnetic susceptibility (Kbulk ) measurements, we propose four mineral exploration indicators for this deposit: (i) the widespread development of kaolinite, dickite, skarn, and retrograde alteration mineral assemblages; (ii) Fe-rich chlorite, identifiable by its characteristic absorption wavelength with Pos2250 > 2250 nm; (iii) white mica–montmorillonite, distinguished by a diagnostic absorption wavelength with Pos2200 1.0 × 10-3SI). Notably, the presence of kaolinite and dickite along the intrusive contact zones between ore-forming porphyries and carbonate wall rocks provides direct evidence for acidic fluid activity during the Cu–Mo–W mineralization process. The development of acid fluid conditions in the system is tightly linked to hydrogen ion release during the precipitation of iron oxides and sulfide minerals. Collectively, these results reveal that hydrothermal fluids associated with Cu–Mo–W mineralization in the Tongshankou deposit evolved from alkaline to acidic throughout the mineralization process. Combined with previous research advances, the present findings further suggest that acidic hydrothermal fluids likely represent a ubiquitous component of skarn-hosted Cu–Mo–W mineralization systems. Clay indicator minerals, including kaolinite and dickite, provide robust constraints on the hydrothermal fluid evolution of Tongshankou deposit and serve as newly recognized, effective exploration proxies for analogous skarn–porphyry Cu–Mo–W deposits worldwide. Significantly, this study demonstrates that the integrated application of SWIR and TIR spectroscopic analyses combined with magnetic susceptibility measurements provides a rigorous and reliable approach for identifying concealed skarn–porphyry ore bodies across the MLYRB, Eastern China. Published: 2026-07-05T22:00:24+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.575 (consider)

item.title: Selective melt extraction of an enriched lithospheric boundary explains rejuvenated OIB
item.dc:creator: Angus Rogers
Oliver Nebel
Hugh O’Neill
Yona Jacobsen
Xueying Wang
Mark A. Kendrick
Fabio Capitanio
Gregory M. Yaxley
item.description: Rejuvenated volcanism on ocean islands represents a resurgence of volcanic activity that postdates mantle plume magmatism, however the mantle source and melting conditions of the generated lavas remain elusive. Aitutaki Island in the central Pacific is an exceptional case of rejuvenated volcanism ∼8 Myr after shield-stage volcanism. Here we present new olivine data, radiogenic isotope and major-trace element data that show a previously undocumented geochemical bimodality in the island’s lavas. Major-trace element data support low-degree melt extraction from the base of the lithosphere, typical of alkalic OIB. Our mineral data and trace element and isotopic modelling indicate an enriched pyroxenitic source fluxed by carbonatitic melt at 3 GPa best reproduces the rejuvenated lavas. This depth of melting corresponds to the breakdown of pargasitic amphibole, and the appearance of a slow seismic velocity anomaly. Our investigation of the rejuvenated lavas from Aitutaki suggests this boundary layer contains enriched pockets of partial melt amongst depleted restite from plume volcanism. Hafnium-Nd systematics imply carbonatite may be a common component of this boundary layer. We argue that under hotter thermal regimes the volatile-depleted restite is more effectively sampled, leading to greater isotopic similarities between shield and rejuvenated lavas than under cooler regimes. Selective sampling of stored components also explains the isotopic kinship between separate islands along a hotspot trace, including at Aitutaki, Rarotonga and Samoa. These results support the presence of a long-lived, enriched partial-melt boundary layer at the base of the lithosphere and show how its reactivation can generate rejuvenated volcanism millions of years after volcanic extinction. Published: 2026-07-09T10:33:25+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.574 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Angus Rogers; Oliver Nebel; Hugh O’Neill; Yona Jacobsen; Xueying Wang; Mark A. Kendrick; Fabio Capitanio; Gregory M. Yaxley

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120221

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

Rejuvenated volcanism on ocean islands represents a resurgence of volcanic activity that postdates mantle plume magmatism, however the mantle source and melting conditions of the generated lavas remain elusive. Aitutaki Island in the central Pacific is an exceptional case of rejuvenated volcanism ∼8 Myr after shield-stage volcanism. Here we present new olivine data, radiogenic isotope and major-trace element data that show a previously undocumented geochemical bimodality in the island’s lavas. This depth of melting corresponds to the breakdown of pargasitic amphibole, and the appearance of a slow seismic velocity anomaly. Our investigation of the rejuvenated lavas from Aitutaki suggests this boundary layer contains enriched pockets of partial melt amongst depleted restite from plume volcanism. Hafnium-Nd systematics imply carbonatite may be a common component of this boundary layer. We argue that under hotter thermal regimes the volatile-depleted restite is more effectively sampled, leading to greater isotopic similarities between shield and rejuvenated lavas than under cooler regimes. Selective sampling of stored components also explains the isotopic kinship between separate islands along a hotspot trace, including at Aitutaki, Rarotonga and Samoa. These results support the presence of a long-lived, enriched partial-melt boundary layer at the base of the lithosphere and show how its reactivation can generate rejuvenated volcanism millions of years after volcanic extinction.
